sentences.text: Yes, they   had fortifications.
Yes.
Of course, the trains used to go to the Eastern Front, and if they disrupt one of those lines, the, the, the German soldiers couldn't get their, their supplies.
And the main task force of all the Partisans and the paratroopers to disrupt the, the transportation to the front.
One day I was selected-- oh, before that, I mean, there was an incident.
There was a camp that was destroyed.
We were in the camp close by, and there was-- they had spies, as they knew it was a German-- a Jewish camp.
And one day in the morning, they attacked that camp.
But we had patrols, so we know that they're coming.
It was a-- it wasn't a regular tower, but we had big trees, tall high trees.
So a man being on a tree could see from a distance that the troops are coming.
And once we could hear the first shot, everybody knew it's time to, to get out and run.
Because we didn't have enough power or guns to fight back.
And to survive, we ran all the way in the woods, maybe a mile away or more.
At the same time, the German and the militia, whatever they call them, came in our camp.
They burned all the whole camp.
The camps were built with pine trees, birch trees.
It's a big hole in the earth, and it's like a triangle.
And that was our accommodation.
So we used to throw in grenades because we're afraid to go in.
We figured maybe, maybe there's dynamite over there.
They didn't know our strengths.
But they know we don't-- we never went in, in open battle with the Germans or, or, or the, or the militia.
And they destroyed our camp.
Two, three miles away, we built another camp.
It was very simple camp.
What is the camp?
A hole, maybe 25 feet by 50.
We put the dirt on the side.
We used to cut off the birch trees and put in four heavy trees on each corner, cover it up with the branches of the trees, and then put in dirt on top.
It was nice and warm.
It was comfortable inside.
That was our accommodation.
If we needed water, we usually built the, the camps on a little bit on a hill.
Even-- it's a wooded area, but there's places in the small hills and low-lying area.
We used to dig a big hole out, let's say, about four by five feet, dig out of there, go down about five feet or less.
And next day we had water, natural water coming from the, from under ground.
And that's the way we got our, our water.
And if you wanted to wash yourself, you took a little bucket we had with us.
And even it's cold outside, 10 degrees below zero, you rinse yourself off, and you, and you run in, back in, in that hiding place.
Now, when I told you about the explosion out there at--
Yes.
Yes, because it's at night.
It's so quiet.
And it's unbelievable.
Even-- any place you go in the woods, if you have pine trees and also birch, and they fall down in little tiny pieces, you step on it, and it makes an awful loud noise.
At that time, we went once, once-- I went with that group.
We had a job to blow up a train with Germans going to the front.
But unfortunately, we didn't have enough dynamite.
So two of us were on both sides, two on each side, and the one who was experienced dig a hole, put in the dynamite.
And when the locomotive-- and we moved back.
And when the locomotive went down the hill, it pulled all the, all the wagons, exploded.
And the locomotive pulled all the other cars.
And it was a slaughter over there of German troops.
But right away, they start shooting.
They didn't know where.
We were already away.
Going back, that was really a good experience, which we-- even we were killing people, but we enjoyed it.
It was the first time we took revenge on troops going to the front.
We came back.
They, they congratulate us.
We, we told them-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] later on we tell them, every one of you get medals.
We didn't care about anything else.
All we want is take revenge.
And life was going on.
Then we had small patrols.
We knew that some of the local people were collaborating with the Nazis.
At night, I used to go in and burn down some of the homes.
There was one problem only, in those small villages, almost every farmer has a dog.
And the dog starts barking at you.
Once one dog barks, all of them.
So they knew that we are coming, the Partisan are coming.
We used to take away the food.
We had never bread every day, but once we, we went in a village like that, we took as much as we could.
Kettle, you brought back in the woods.
Flour, salt was very important.
We never had enough.
And the farmers used to have because when you slaughter a cow, the only way you could preserve it is they had barrels.
They put the meat, the meat in those wooden barrels and put a lot of salt, and that was buried in the ground.
And that's the way we preserved the meat, so we had food for the next week or two or four.
Yes, well, clothing was not uniforms.
We didn't have any Russian or German-- once in a while we had some German uniforms.
But most of the time, we had civilian clothes, which I had it from two years ago, the same shirt, the same jacket.
If somebody went on, on, on patrol or, or a watchtower, it was considered a watchtower, we, we used to-- when you exchange the, the, the, the people, we used to use the same coat, fur coat.
But as far as, as clothing, the same shirt, the same thing, the same shoes you had from the last year and a half or two years.
If you were torn out, we used to have, you know, birch, the skin, that when it is wet, you can use it almost like rubberized.
We used to put them around our old torn-up shoes or old shoes, take a piece of rag on top, put a little water on top.
In the wintertime, used to freeze up.
You never took it off, your shoes.
And that's the way we were walking around.
And as far as guns, we had-- there was always a shortage.
If somebody went on patrol, we had, let's say, 10 or 15 people, we had guns.
But the camp was almost without guns.
But if we, if we caught a militia man or a German, we used to take, naturally, take the uniforms, take the guns, take as much as we wanted.
And over the years, over the weeks and months, we, we acquired more guns.
And lately in end of 19 3, winter 19 3 and beginning of '94, the early part--
1943, I'm sorry, '43 and beginning of 1944, the-- some of the Partisans can't contact already with regular army.
They used to parachute with packages, actually boxes with ammunition and guns.
There was no communication, I mean.
I remember once we were waiting for a, for a plane to drop us some, some guns and ammunition, and only-- it looks like somebody had some contact with the army.
They made a big fire in, in the woods, like an envelope, you made two, four, five, five fires when you are in the air and you're in the middle of the woods.
There were some places that were, that were actually-- there were no-- nothing was growing.
They could see the fire.
And once they dropped us some, some guns.
And we had ammunition, and we had also dynamite.
But it was very risky.
Many times they threw it down, and we never got it.
The contacts were, were very bad.
I don't know what it is.
I mean, even the ex-soldiers or ex-officers, they did not have good contact.
But later, when they had some paratroopers, they threw it on also paratroopers in, in the area, that they had better contact with the, with the front lines.
If they wanted to catch somebody, once in a while, they will catch a high officer, a German officer.
They brought them to the front.
They went through the front line.
They brought them over on the Soviet side.
Yes.
On a daily basis, we had the password for a day.
But we knew each other.
It's not that you had a big army that if you don't know your password, you get killed or shot or whatever.
So they used, they used-- we used to know each other.
But we had-- every day it was changed.
No, we did not run.
Only time we run was they had-- we had already contacts with the, with the farmers.
And if they expected or we know that extra troops came into the area, so we were prepared already that they might, they might try to encircle us.
They tried many times to do it, but they were not successful.
They, they, they, they come in the camp.
What's over there?
There was nothing in the camp actually, except holes covered up, living quarters.
We, we captured some of them, but there was no interrogation.
We just finished them up on, on the spot and took the guns, took the shoes, took off the clothes, and back to the camp.
As, as far as we are concerned, we never, never ask any questions.
As long as he was in, in a German, German uniform, that was the end of him.
Yes.
Look, where we shooting, when we shoot one man, all of us were shooting.
Nevertheless, if you had four or five or six people, everybody used their gun, no questions asked.
No, we couldn't say, I did it or he did it.
Most of the time we all-- we were so outraged that we didn't care.
And the German, I mean, maybe he was just a regular German or a soldier.
He was-- but we did not care.
As long as we saw the uniform with a Nazi marking, that was the end of him.
The coats we were using.
We take off, yes.
The, the coats, the shoes, of course, was very short on shoes.
But also we had the problem with using German shoes, because all of us were almost half barefoot.
When you are using the military shoes, you making too much noise.
So the shoes, we used to leave in, in the camp.
But the uniforms, yes, we used to use them, tear off the insignias and wear them.
The farmers, they didn't know what to say.
Are we Germans?
Are we Partisans?
But once we started talking to them, they calm down, because they were awful afraid.
What about if, if someone was hurt or badly, badly hurt.
Injured.
No, no.
No, there's no such, no such, such, thing as going in for help.
Because once you going to help, you never come out alive.
We used to-- if somebody got injured, we used to carry him to the camp.
Now, there was very little medicines or any medical help.
We had one doctor in our place, but unfortunately, he didn't have no medications.
So when we used to go into the farm, we used to go and ask him, do you have any, any medicines?
And as soon as we find anything, we thought that might have something, we didn't wait for that person should tell us.
We just turn it upside down, the toilet or whatever, and we used to take it away.
Because we didn't have no, no medical help at all.
There was one place, deep in the woods where they kept some very badly injured Partisans.
They were away from the camps.
They couldn't even walk.
They had to amputate a leg.
They used a butcher knife.
Either the man survived or he died on, on, on the primitive table.
There was no medications.
We didn't get anything.
Once in a while, if we were lucky, we got in, in a bigger village where they had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] militia, where  they had a headquarter of Germans.
And we got in, and actually, we used to be lucky and get some-- steal it actually, or take it or whatever you want to call it-- from the enemy.
But from, from a parachute or anything else, we never got anything.
And the doctor used primitive medicine.
Not primitive medicine-- he didn't have anything [INAUDIBLE].
It does not [INAUDIBLE] as an aspirin or anything else.
We didn't know about it.
Yes.
It was a place where they kept it.
I wouldn't call it a, a field hospital.
Because there was no beds, no medicine, primitive.
They used to use some herbs from the woods over there.
I don't know what they were using.
They used to get a little better food, but a lot of them died on, on the operating.
There was once a case, I remember, in our camp that the man got the bullet and they had to take off his leg.
And they cut off his leg with a butcher knife.
No medicine, nothing.
And this was going on all the time until 1944.
In the Partisan, no, but later on [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in the summer of 1944, when we knew the Russian army was going west.
In fact, at night, we could, we could hear the explosion from the bombs.
It's the funniest thing.
I mean, if you lay down on the ground at night when it is so quiet, you can hear the that  the earth is shaking almost like an earthquake.
And every night, everybody was laying down and listening, and we could hear that the explosions are getting closer and closer.

It's possible that the paratroopers knew about it, and they tried-- they-- not it was possible, they knew for sure, because to try to disrupt the evacuation of the German army they used to blow up the, the highways.
They had the poles, you know, telephone poles.
We used to go out at night, maybe 20 people, with saw and cut off the poles and the-- and cut the, the telephone wires so there was no, no, no connection.
I don't know.
Maybe at that time they didn't have it yet, the cellular phones, it looks like.
Because we were cutting it almost on a daily basis.
In the morning, it was a tank in the front.
The, the farmers used to tell us that the Germans came in a tank and infantry behind and they would try to fix those poles.
It looks like they were completely disrupted.
Either they had different communication, we didn't know.
But the Partisans did a lot of, a lot of trouble for, for the German army.
And later on, the civilian population had expected the Russian [INAUDIBLE], they were more friendly to us.
There was a time they were really miserable.
If they saw a Partisan, they didn't even talk to you.
Even at night, having a gun in my hand, I was so frightened because I didn't know which way to go.
And--
Yes.
Young women.
Yes, I would say, three to one or four to one approximately.
No, no, they-- mostly they were in the camp.
There was once an incident, I just remind myself.
There was a young couple.
They had a, a young child, maybe a year, year and a half old, approximately.
I don't know, maybe two years.
And we know we're going to be encircled.
We expected it.
And the commander told her, you-- either you take your child and go out by yourself or you got something to do about it.
Or we take away the child.
And believe it or not, they gave up the child.
And they went away and, and, and in a stack of hay they put in the baby.
And one of the officers shot the baby and left him inside.
They told them, either it was the baby or all of us were going to get killed.
We didn't have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] enough guns to, to defend ourself.
Nobody worried about it that we might get killed.
It never came to my mind that I might get killed.
All, all we want to do is just kill the Germans.
And, and finally it ended in 1944.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Russian military came in.
We were in-- we were a distance from Baranovich.
And there was a call, probably the paratroopers had it that we have to protect-- there was a bridge somehow so the Russian tanks can go through.
They told them after that, it wasn't a joke.
I, I thought it looks like a joke almost that we have to try to protect that bridge.
The Germans should not blow it up.
Because if it blow up, the tanks cannot go forward.
And believe it or not, we were close to the bridge, laying in a ditch.
There was a few hundred men at least.
Everybody had the rifle.
No machine guns, just rifles.
And Germans would try to blow it up.
And they all got killed.
And then, maybe two hours later, Russian tanks came over.
They didn't know that we are Partisans and figured they'd start shooting at us.
There was no communication.
But then one had to open the door or something on the side or whatever it is.
He yelled to me, tovarish, tovarish, you know, comrade.
And they realized that we are Partisans.
And at that time, the tanks went forward.
All the Partisans stopped going forward.
We just let the regular army take care.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we all got in back to the city.
Suddenly, we saw thousands of people with machine guns, with pistols, rifles, all kind of uniforms, German uniforms, local police uniforms.
We all got together.
We start marching into the city, and there was representative [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from the Russian army that greeted us.
And we told them, now you can rest overnight.
We'll give you some new uniforms.
We joined the regular Russian army.
In our group was approximately maybe 110, 120, 130 maximum.
There was one, which I remembered, he was one commander.
His name was Gorbachev.
Gorbachev.
Yes.
No, no, that's just a, a, a regular Russian name.
It looks like he was an officer from the, from the regular Russian army.
And he never surrendered.
And he organized a lot of people.
You know that he was with us.
And he helped us a lot.
There were some more Russian ex-soldiers.
They were giving us some training, how to use a gun.
Now, we're training, we did not shoot.
Because we had to save the bullets.
We didn't have enough bullets.
But how to handle a gun, how to take apart and clean the gun, of course, we didn't know about it.
And it helped us a lot.
When we came in, they also showed us how to operate machine guns.
We had the old-fashioned machine guns.
I don't know if you saw it in the movies with big disk, and it was a lot of bullets.
One was carrying the-- had, had the bullets on himself, and the other one was the machine gunner.
That's all before the, the Russian army came in.
Well, you see first of all, we are sleeping in the same quarters, so no man or woman were alone.
We all sleep in the same bunk.
We had-- we didn't think about it.
We were close relation we had.
You know, by the name, we [INAUDIBLE].
I mean, we used to go on, on the patrols.
We used to tell them be careful.
We know this like, like girlfriends or boyfriends, but there was no close love relations.
Maybe somebody else did, but in my case, I was too young.
I was naive.
I was a virgin.
I didn't know what it means, a woman yet.
There were a few of them were ex-Russian soldiers.
And they were the leaders of our group.
One of them had a young Jewish woman.
He was living with her.
This-- everybody to know about it.
And after the war, he, he wanted she should go with him to Moscow.
He went to marry.
What happens, I don't know, because when we come-- when the Russian Army came into Baranovich, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we rest almost a day or two.
We, we start looking around like human being.
So I mean, it was unbelievable.
You see people from all over, not only Jews you met, a lot of Russian soldiers you met.
And we all were, the next day, we were all had to register ourself, our names.
And we got official Russian uniforms.
And we were drafted in the service in the Russian army.
OK, go ahead.
Yes.
We had to be very careful.
We never walked alone.
You always were at least three or four people at one time.
And at least two of us had guns.
There was an incident once.
We were close to a village, but it was the area occupied.
It was also called-- or under, under the control from the Partisans.
And one of them, they came over.
Two guys all had guns.
And we were walking just, just like you would be at home.
And he said, you give us the guns.
I jumped.
And I got in the ditch, and I moved my, my, my, my gun.
And I told him, if you don't get out, you get killed right now.
They took off fast.
It's the only incident I had.
You Jews don't need guns.
